title: verify.ts reintroduces the global-mutable seam A13 was meant to retire
description: Cycle 4 explicitly killed the module-level setSpawnOverride/spawnOverrides Map in preflight.ts (A13, M005 carry-forward) in favor of per-call ProbeOptions.spawn injection. verify.ts then introduces the exact same anti-pattern in production code: a module-level let _verifyOverride and an exported setVerifyOverride() that the harness installs at boot. The contract specified the verify module should follow the probe pattern ('Spawn injection seam parallels the probe pattern (opts.spawn?: SpawnFn)'), and runVerification already accepts opts.spawn. The override branch sits in front of the spawn-injection branch and silently shadows it, so callers passing opts.spawn while a stub is installed get the stub instead. This is the very design seam A13 was about — moved one file over.
impact: Inconsistent design across verify.ts vs preflight.ts; reviewers/auditors must track two distinct test-seam patterns; the production import surface still leaks a global-mutable test hook (the synthesis-notes flagged this as carry-forward 'A13 intent partially met by renaming'); cycle 5 is set to add 3 more verification adapters, each of which would be expected to follow whichever pattern is canonical, and right now the canonical answer is contradictory.
recommendation: Drop _verifyOverride / setVerifyOverride. Have withVerifyStub install the stub through the same harness-internal map pattern preflight uses (key on something stable like 'compile' or just pass through verifyOptions at the harness's verifySpot wrapper boundary), and route the stub via runVerification(opts.spawn) — the existing opts.spawn branch already handles synthetic results when the spawn fn returns { status: 0, ... }. Or: thread VerifyOptions through verifySpot's tool entry so the harness can pass opts at call time, parallel to how runProbe already takes ProbeOptions.

title: RegistryWarning union ships 11 kinds, contract specified exactly 8
description: Contract A12 mandates RegistryWarning be a true discriminated union of EXACTLY the eight kinds registry.ts emits ('no-paths-dir', 'empty-paths-dir', 'missing-path-json', 'malformed-path-json', 'invalid-path-json', 'missing-phases-json', 'malformed-phases-json', 'invalid-phases-json'). The shipped union has 11 members — three additional 'semantic alias' kinds ('paths-missing', 'paths-empty', 'path-malformed') exist purely to keep cycle-3-era preflight.test.ts strings green. registry.ts emits ZERO of those three. This is the same shape (orphan members the producer never emits) that cycle 3 R1-003/R2-001/M002 flagged on the prior loose '{kind:string}' shape — only now those orphans are encoded as named literal members rather than uninhabited interfaces. Narrowing on `if (w.kind === 'paths-missing')` infers a non-empty type from the producer's perspective, which is wrong.
impact: The discriminated-union promise (caller can narrow to known producer outputs) is undermined: code branching on 'paths-missing' will TS-typecheck but is dead at runtime; future contributors who see `paths-missing` in the union will assume registry.ts can emit it; the carry-forward debt M002 is technically still open. The synthesis-notes flagged this in worker comparison ('worker-6 over-built EngineWarning (19 kinds vs 16 specified)') but the chosen worker still over-built by 3.
recommendation: Drop the three alias members from RegistryWarning. Update tests/preflight.test.ts (lines 529-531, 569; tests/registry.test.ts lines 206, 577) — those are filesystem-fixture path strings (`fixtures/paths-empty`), not kind literals; the test's grep was matching directory names, not warning kinds. If the test genuinely asserts those literals as union members, the test is wrong (tests should track the contract, not the implementation's accidental over-inclusion).

title: buildPrompts/declaredOptions logic duplicated across selectPath and setPersonalization
description: selectPath.ts:24-54 (buildPrompts) and setPersonalization.ts:54-79 (declaredOptions construction) implement the same loop: iterate pathData.personalization_options, branch on 'poll_interval_ms' vs 'pool_subset', fall back to identical hardcoded defaults ({min:1000,max:30000,default:3000} and {values:['both','DEEP_SUI','SUI_USDC'],default:'both'}). The two implementations differ only in the output type (Prompt vs PersonalizationOptionDecl), but the shapes overlap nearly entirely (both have name/type/range/enum/default). When cycle 5+ adds a third personalization option the maintainer must remember to edit both files, and either's drift (e.g. a min/max change in one but not the other) introduces a hard-to-spot bug where validate accepts what selectPath rejects (or vice versa).
impact: Drift hazard between the prompt the skill renders and the validator's acceptance set. Adds ~50 LOC of redundancy in scope of contract that called this out as design-sensitive (contract: 'reviewers will grep for any other consumer touching target_file/target_range'). Maintenance multiplier per new personalization option = 2.
recommendation: Extract a single `buildDeclaredOptions(pathData) → PersonalizationOptionDecl[]` in personalization.ts (or a new tools/_pathOptions.ts helper). selectPath maps DeclaredOptionDecl → Prompt for the wire shape; setPersonalization passes the array straight to validatePersonalizationValues. Single source of truth for option metadata; new options need exactly one site edit.

title: Four tools repeat the same state-load preamble inline
description: selectPath, setPersonalization, nextSpot, verifySpot each open with the same 5-line idiom: loadState; if corrupt → error; if schema-mismatch → error; if absent or no selected_path → error. Three of them additionally repeat the path.json-load + validate block (selectPath.ts:92-105, setPersonalization.ts:38-51 are byte-identical aside from a comment). The phase-loading block (loadPhases + try/catch wrapping the LoadPhasesError) is repeated verbatim in nextSpot.ts:67-73 and verifySpot.ts:33-39. Four small tools share four duplicated preludes; new tools (cycle 5+) will paste the same prologue.
impact: Each new phase-loop tool inherits ~20 lines of boilerplate before its actual logic. Changes to the state-load contract (e.g. add a new 'absent-but-recoverable' kind, surface state-corrupt warnings on the wire) require touching four sites. Inconsistencies are likely (e.g., setPersonalization uses falsy `!stateResult.state.selected_path` while selectPath checks `stateResult.kind === 'ok' ? stateResult.state : null` — different truthiness semantics for empty-string).
recommendation: Add `mcp/server/src/tools/_phaseToolBase.ts` exposing `loadPhaseToolContext(projectRoot)` that returns either `{ ok: true; state, slug, pathData, phasesData }` or a structured error. Each tool calls it as a one-liner, then does its actual work. Cycle 5+ verb tools (askForHint, showReference, autoWrite) plug in trivially.

title: validatePhases dual-mode (stub vs full) softens contract A2's tightening
description: Contract A2 says 'Tighten validatePhases so each spot must declare target_file/target_range/prompt/verification...' . The shipped validator detects whether a spot has 'any' of those fields (hasAnyNewField) and only enforces all-four when one is present; otherwise it silently accepts a stub spot with just `{id, title?}`. This bifurcates the schema into two implicit modes that callers have to know about: nextSpot has its own runtime guard (lines 84-91) re-checking the four fields and emits 'Spot is a stub spot (incomplete fields); cannot render', because the schema let it through. The synthesis-notes called out worker-3's dual-validation split as 'an interesting design decision' that worker-4 chose otherwise — but worker-4 actually shipped a different dual mode (one validator, two acceptance sets). The placeholder phases 2/3 in 01-orderbook-viewer/phases.json could equally well have been authored as full-spec spots with `verification.mode: 'compile'` + a sentinel command + a 'TBD in cycle 5' prompt — exactly what the contract's 'placeholder' language implied.
impact: Schema is no longer a strong invariant: a manifest can ship a spot that the engine cannot serve, and the failure surfaces only at nextSpot/verifySpot call time as a runtime error string instead of a registry-scan-time validation warning (defeating part of the cycle-3 R&R purpose of having registry vouch for both files together — registry.ts:106-142 calls validatePhases but the validator passes manifests the engine itself rejects). Cycle 5 will add the missing phase-2/3 spots and may forget to re-tighten, leaving a permanent 'stub allowed' loophole.
recommendation: Either (a) actually require all four fields per the contract, and migrate phases 2/3 placeholders to full spots with a sentinel verification.command (e.g., `echo TBD; exit 1`), or (b) introduce an explicit `placeholder: true` flag on the spot that the schema and runtime guards both consult, so the two-mode reality is encoded in the data instead of inferred from field presence. Today's 'hasAnyNewField' inference is brittle — adding a fifth optional field (e.g. cycle-5 doc_link inline content) means the placeholder/full boundary moves.

title: verifySpot casts spot.verification through unknown — schema invariant not type-tracked
description: verifySpot does `const verSpec = (spot.verification as unknown) as VerificationSpec;` after a runtime undefined check. The double-cast through unknown is the TS-strict idiom for forcing a coercion the compiler refuses, which here is needed only because SpotData declares `verification?: VerificationSpec` (optional, R2-005). The runtime guard at line 50 narrows `spot.verification !== undefined` but the local binding `spot` is typed as SpotData with verification still optional — so TS rightly complains. The cast is benign at runtime but tells the next maintainer 'I know better than the type system' which is exactly backwards: the type system is right; the underlying schema design is wrong (R2-005). The synthesis-notes flagged this exact cast as a round-1 failure cluster ('TS strict (verifySpot.ts cast)').
impact: Future schema changes that genuinely break VerificationSpec compatibility will not be caught at this site — the cast laundromat erases TS's check. Code-review noise, plus a small invitation to copy-paste the same pattern to other tool sites.
recommendation: If R2-005 lands and SpotData's full-spot mode is enforced, this cast disappears: the runtime check `if (spot.verification === undefined)` against a non-optional SpotData['verification']: VerificationSpec becomes a no-op TS recognises. Short-term fix without R2-005: introduce a type guard `function isFullSpot(s: SpotData): s is Required<Pick<SpotData,'target_file'|'target_range'|'prompt'|'verification'>> & SpotData` so the narrow happens in the type system.
evidence: // Cast through unknown to satisfy TS strict — spot.verification is a VerificationSpec\n  // as validated by the schema (validatePhases runtime check above)\n  const verSpec = (spot.verification as unknown) as VerificationSpec;

title: advanceCursor uses string sentinels '__done__' for done-marker — leaks into state.cursor on disk
description: When the cursor walks past the last spot, advanceCursor sets `cursor: { phase_id: '__done__', spot_id: '__done__' }` and relies on getCurrentSpot's missing-id branch to return `{ done: true }`. Then verifySpot.ts:62-64 calls saveState with this sentinel state, persisting `'__done__'` strings to the on-disk state.json. Three problems: (1) the sentinel is colocated with real cursor data — a malicious or buggy phases.json that names a phase '__done__' silently advances the cursor; (2) the State schema (state.ts) treats cursor.{phase_id, spot_id} as plain strings, so a future schema-validate pass cannot distinguish 'cursor is done' from 'cursor was never set'; (3) the only signal of done-ness is the absence of a matching phase id in the manifest, which means renaming a phase between cycles silently re-activates a previously-completed cursor.
impact: On-disk state file contains opaque sentinels surfacing in any debug log, support bundle, or future migration tool. Cross-cycle resumption logic (cycle 5+) must learn the sentinel convention. A colliding phase id is a low-probability footgun but the cost of a structured marker (e.g., `cursor: null` or a separate `state.done: boolean`) is one schema bump and trivial validator-side normalization.
recommendation: Add an explicit `done: boolean` to State (or change cursor to `Cursor | null`). advanceCursor returns `{ ...state, cursor: null, done: true }` past the last spot. validateState normalizes cursor.phase_id/spot_id non-empty when cursor is non-null. getCurrentSpot reads `state.done` first. Schema bump can wait for cycle 5 (when other State fields evolve), but at minimum reserve the field name and document the sentinel until then.

title: selectPath redundantly re-reads/re-validates path.json after scanRegistry already did
description: scanRegistry (registry.ts) reads, parses, validates, and shapes path.json into PathInfo. selectPath then calls scanRegistry, finds the slug, and immediately re-reads + re-parses + re-validates the same path.json (selectPath.ts:94-102) just to get personalization_ranges — a field PathInfo's projection happens to drop. Same pattern in setPersonalization.ts:38-51. The fix is to widen PathInfo to include personalization_ranges (already in PathData and validated), or to expose a `getValidatedPath(scanRoot, slug)` from registry.ts that returns the full PathData. Two file reads + two JSON.parse + two validatePath calls per tool invocation, all serving the same byte stream.
impact: Minor performance cost (negligible at one tool call per second) but doubled error-handling surface: each tool now has two failure modes for the same file ('registry rejected path.json' and 'tool re-validation rejected path.json'), and the messages differ. Maintenance cost when path.json adds fields: every tool must remember to extend its local validation block to surface them.
recommendation: Promote `personalization_ranges` from PathData to PathInfo (or add a richer `RegistryEntry` type with the full validated PathData). Update scanRegistry to populate it. selectPath/setPersonalization read the registry once and project what they need.

title: State.selected_path is required-string in schema but used as optional everywhere
description: validateState requires selected_path to be a string (line 45-47). All four cycle-4 tools then test `!stateResult.state.selected_path` — i.e. treat empty-string as 'not selected'. The semantics across the codebase: 'selected_path is set' = 'state.kind === ok && state.selected_path !== ""'. But validateState accepts `""` as valid; nothing in saveState rejects an empty string; the state file thus has two ways to encode 'no path' (file absent → kind:absent, vs file present with selected_path: "" → kind:ok with falsy slug) and the four tools handle them via two different branches.
impact: A buggy or malicious state.json with selected_path: "" passes schema validation, slips past selectPath/setPersonalization/nextSpot/verifySpot's 'No path selected' branch the same way 'absent' would, but bypasses scanRegistry's slug-validation gate that selectPath's normal flow runs. nextSpot then tries `loadPhases(projectRoot, '')` and surfaces a fopen error instead of a clean 'no path selected'. Wire-protocol-equivalent failure modes are spelled differently to the caller.
recommendation: Either: make selected_path: `string | null` in the State type and update validateState accordingly (and tests' makeValidState helpers); OR validate selected_path.length > 0 in validateState and emit a single canonical 'state shape invalid' classification. The cycle-2 C008 'state-shape-invalid' kind carry-forward already maps to this exact case.
evidence: if (typeof obj['selected_path'] !== 'string') {\n    return { ok: false, error: 'selected_path must be a string' };\n  }

title: Personalization OptionDesc has two parallel shapes — DeclaredOptions union accepts both
description: personalization.ts exports two parallel descriptor shapes: `PersonalizationOptionDecl` (with `range?: {min,max,default}` and `enum?: string[]` — the schema/wire shape) and `OptionDesc` (with flattened `min/max` directly on the integer variant — a 'test suite and flat option descriptor' shape per the comment). validatePersonalizationValues accepts either via `DeclaredOptions = PersonalizationOptionDecl[] | Record<string, OptionDesc>` and a `normalizeToArray` helper. Two formats for the same semantic content, with the converter dropping/relabeling fields between them. The 'normalize to array' branch handles the Record path used by tests; the array path handles tools' callers. This is two coexisting input shapes carrying identical information, only one of which the schema actually defines.
impact: API surface area doubled with no behavioral payoff. Tests grow attached to the Record shape; future refactors that rename PersonalizationOptionDecl can break tests via the OptionDesc indirection. The synthesis-notes mention test-fixture amendments to support shape transitions; this fork is one source of fragility.
recommendation: Pick one canonical shape (PersonalizationOptionDecl, since it matches path.json's serialization). Update tests to construct option arrays directly. Drop OptionDesc + normalizeToArray (~25 LOC) in favor of a single signature `validatePersonalizationValues(values, options: PersonalizationOptionDecl[])`. If the test ergonomic 'Record<string, OptionDesc>' matters, expose a tiny `optionsFromRecord(rec)` helper in tests' test-utils, not in production personalization.ts.
evidence: // Accept either an array of PersonalizationOptionDecl or a Record<string, OptionDesc>\ntype DeclaredOptions = PersonalizationOptionDecl[] | Record<string, OptionDesc>;
